text:
{
  "instructions":"Write your names and index number on the answer booklet as they appear on your registration form, and DO NOT write your names and index number on additional answer sheets of paper if provided. Do not open this paper until you are told to do so. This paper consists of three sections A, B and C. Answer ALL questions in section A. Answer THREE questions in section B. Answer only one question in section C. Use only a blue or black pen.",
   "answerKey":{
      "1":"a) Organelle, cell, tissue, organ, organ system; b) i. Organism; ii. Organism (or cell); iii. Organ; iv. Organelle; v. Organ system",
      "2":"a) Receptor cells; b) Sensory neurons; c) Motor neurons",
      "3":"a) Larva stage and adult stage; b) Larva stage; c) Pupa stage; d) Pupa stage",
      "4":"Drinking bottled water, proper sanitation, proper disposal of wastes, covering food, washing hands before eating",
      "5":"a) Photosynthesis is a metabolic process by which plants and other autotrophic organisms make their own food using water and carbon dioxide in the presence of sunlight and chlorophyll. b) Conditions necessary for photosynthesis: presence of water, carbon dioxide, suitable temperature, presence of chlorophyll or chloroplasts",
      "6":"a) Magnesium, iron; b) i. In green plants: magnesium is required in the synthesis of chlorophyll; ii. In mammals: iron is used in the synthesis of haemoglobin",
      "7":"a) Community: A number of species interacting in a locality; b) Ecosystem: Interaction of organisms with each other and with their abiotic environment; c) Food web: A nutritional interrelationship of organisms; d) Habitat: A place where an organism lives",
      "8":"a) Enzymes are frequently referred to as biological catalysts because they speed up the rate of chemical reactions in living organisms. b) Changing the pH of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum pH at which they work. When the pH is changed, the enzymes change shape so that their substrate cannot fit in their active sites. This is called enzyme denaturation. c) Changing the temperature of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum temperature at which they work. When the temperature is changed, the enzymes will be denatured",
      "9":"a) A: vena cava; B: aorta; C: hepatic vein; D: hepatic artery; E: hepatic portal vein; b) Glucose, amino acids",
      "10":"Thinking, reasoning, storage of information, memory, love, imagination, vision",
      "11":"It is a reflex action. A reflex action is an automatic response to a stimulus which is not under the control of the brain. Reflexes are controlled by the spinal cord. The command from the spinal cord makes the muscles of the hand to contract, removing the hand from the hot object (stimulus)",
      "12":"a) A gamete is a cell involved in sexual reproduction. b) The male gamete is the sperm. The female gamete is the egg",
      "13":"a) i. Alleles are different forms of the same gene. ii. Heterozygous: having different alleles for a given character. iii. Phenotype: the physical appearance of an organism. b) Brown eye is dominant: B; Blue eyes is recessive: b. Cross between two parents: one heterozygous (Bb) and the other with blue eyes (bb). i. Genotype of the brown-eyed parent: Bb. ii. The possible gametes that each parent can produce: B and b from the parent with genotype Bb and b from the parent with genotype bb. iii. The possible genotypes of their children: 50% Bb; 50% bb",
      "14":"a) i. Biosphere is the place on Earth where life is possible. ii. Habitat is the place where an organism lives. iii. Niche is the role of the species in the ecosystem. iv. Producer: is an organism that can make its own food. b) i. Ash tree — Caterpillars — Robins — Owls; ii. Ash tree — Earthworms — Robins — Owls. Autotrophic organism: Ash tree. Secondary consumer: Robins, ladybirds. Omnivore: Robins. Carnivore: Owls",
      "15":"a) The failure of the pancreas to produce sufficient insulin will lead to a disorder called diabetes mellitus. b) i. Growth hormone (GH), TSH, ACTH, prolactin, FSH, LH. Growth hormone (GH): Stimulates growth especially of bones and limbs and metabolic functions. TSH: Synthesis and secretion of thyroid hormones, growth of thyroid glands. ACTH: Stimulates the adrenal cortex to secrete its hormones. Prolactin: stimulates milk production and secretion. FSH: In male, stimulates spermatogenesis. In female, growth of ovarian follicles. LH: In male, testosterone production. In female, secretion of oestrogens and progesterone, ovulation and maintenance of corpus luteum",
      "16":"a) A: vagina; B: cervix; C: amniotic sac; D: uterus; E: placenta; F: oviduct; G: foetus. b) (i): Growth of an ovarian follicle. (ii): The ovary ruptures to release an egg (ovulation). (iii): Sperm are introduced in the vagina during sexual intercourse. (iv): The egg moves toward the oviduct. (v): The sperm meets the egg and one of the sperm fertilizes the egg forming a diploid zygote. The zygote spends the next few days traveling down the Fallopian tube. As it travels, it divides by mitosis several times to form a ball of cells called a morula. The cell divisions, which are called cleavage, increase the number of cells but not their overall size. More cell divisions occur, and soon a fluid-filled cavity forms inside the ball of cells. At this stage, the ball of cells is called a blastocyst. (vi): The blastocyst embeds in the wall of the uterus. This is called implantation",
      "17":"a) i. Sepals: protection of inner parts of the flower (reproductive organs). ii. Petals: attract pollinators such as bees and insects. b) Self-pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anthers to the stigma of the same flower. Cross-pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anthers of one flower to the stigma of another flower of another plant of the same species. c) Are scented (have a pleasant strong smell). Are large and conspicuous (very noticeable or tending to attract attention). Pollen grains are rough, sticky on insect’s body. Are brightly coloured. Produce nectar. d) The seedling obtains materials and energy from its growth from the endosperm. The endosperm contains storage molecules which are hydrolysed into simple monomers to be used by the seedling before it starts producing food by its own",
      "18":"a) A diagram showing salt molecules that are moving from the bottom upwards (salt molecules are moving from their highest concentration to their lowest concentration). A diagram showing salt molecules with a uniform distribution in the water. iii. Diffusion. b) Membrane. Water molecules have moved from hypotonic solution to hypertonic solution through a selectively permeable membrane. This is because water always moves from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane. iii. Osmosis. iv. Osmosis is a movement of water molecules from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane"
   },
   "allowSelectiveAnswering":true,
   "sectionBRequiredQuestions":3,
   "sectionCRequiredQuestions":1,
   "sections":[
      {
         "name":"A",
         "description":"Attempt all questions from this section. (55 marks)",
         "questions":[
            {
               "questionNumber":1,
               "text":"The following are various sub-units into which species of plants or animals can be divided: cell, organ, organelle, organism, tissue, organ system.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Arrange them in order starting with the simplest and ending with the most complex.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Organelle, cell, tissue, organ, organ system",
                     "points":3
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"Which of them can be applied to the following:",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "subQuestions":[
                        {
                           "label":"i)",
                           "text":"A cat?",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Organism",
                           "points":1
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"ii)",
                           "text":"Amoeba?",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Organism (or cell)",
                           "points":1
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"iii)",
                           "text":"Leaf?",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Organ",
                           "points":1
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"iv)",
                           "text":"Chloroplast?",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Organelle",
                           "points":1
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"v)",
                           "text":"Alimentary canal?",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Organ system",
                           "points":1
                        }
                     ],
                     "correctAnswer":"i) Organism; ii) Organism (or cell); iii) Organ; iv) Organelle; v) Organ system",
                     "points":5
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) Organelle, cell, tissue, organ, organ system; b) i. Organism; ii. Organism (or cell); iii. Organ; iv. Organelle; v. Organ system",
               "points":8
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":2,
               "text":"Name the cells in a mammal that:",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Are sensitive to their environment.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Receptor cells",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"Receive messages from sensory cells,",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Sensory neurons",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"c)",
                     "text":"Transmit messages to effectors.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Motor neurons",
                     "points":1
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) Receptor cells; b) Sensory neurons; c) Motor neurons",
               "points":3
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":3,
               "text":"Name the stage in the life cycle of flies and butterflies at which:",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Ecdysis occurs.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Larva stage and adult stage",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"Feeding for growth occurs.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Larva stage",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"c)",
                     "text":"Wings appear,",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Pupa stage",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"d)",
                     "text":"A period of immobility occurs.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Pupa stage",
                     "points":1
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) Larva stage and adult stage; b) Larva stage; c) Pupa stage; d) Pupa stage",
               "points":4
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":4,
               "text":"Cholera is transmitted by food and water that is contaminated with faecal matter. Suggest three measures that might be used to limit the spread of this disease.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "correctAnswer":"Drinking bottled water, proper sanitation, proper disposal of wastes, covering food, washing hands before eating",
               "points":3
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":5,
               "text":"a) Define photosynthesis. b) What conditions are necessary for photosynthesis to occur in a green plant?",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Define photosynthesis.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Photosynthesis is a metabolic process by which plants and other autotrophic organisms make their own food using water and carbon dioxide in the presence of sunlight and chlorophyll",
                     "points":2
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"What conditions are necessary for photosynthesis to occur in a green plant?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Presence of water, carbon dioxide, suitable temperature, presence of chlorophyll or chloroplasts",
                     "points":4
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) Photosynthesis is a metabolic process by which plants and other autotrophic organisms make their own food using water and carbon dioxide in the presence of sunlight and chlorophyll. b) Presence of water, carbon dioxide, suitable temperature, presence of chlorophyll or chloroplasts",
               "points":6
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":6,
               "text":"a) List two chemical elements other than carbon, hydrogen, oxygen and nitrogen which are required by both plants and animals. b) For each of the elements you have listed, give one reason why it is required:",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"List two chemical elements other than carbon, hydrogen, oxygen and nitrogen which are required by both plants and animals.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Magnesium, iron",
                     "points":2
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"For each of the elements you have listed, give one reason why it is required:",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "subQuestions":[
                        {
                           "label":"i)",
                           "text":"In green plants.",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Magnesium is required in the synthesis of chlorophyll",
                           "points":1
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"ii)",
                           "text":"In mammals.",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Iron is used in the synthesis of haemoglobin",
                           "points":1
                        }
                     ],
                     "correctAnswer":"i) Magnesium is required in the synthesis of chlorophyll; ii) Iron is used in the synthesis of haemoglobin",
                     "points":2
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) Magnesium, iron. b) i) Magnesium is required in the synthesis of chlorophyll; ii) Iron is used in the synthesis of haemoglobin",
               "points":4
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":7,
               "text":"Match the following ecological terms with their definitions.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Community",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"A number of species interacting in a locality",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"Ecosystem",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Interaction of organisms with each other and with their abiotic environment",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"c)",
                     "text":"Food web",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"A nutritional interrelationship of organisms",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"d)",
                     "text":"Habitat",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"A place where an organism lives",
                     "points":1
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) Community: A number of species interacting in a locality; b) Ecosystem: Interaction of organisms with each other and with their abiotic environment; c) Food web: A nutritional interrelationship of organisms; d) Habitat: A place where an organism lives",
               "points":4
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":8,
               "text":"a) Why are enzymes frequently referred to as ‘biological catalysts'? b) What would be the effect of changing: i) pH? ii) Temperature upon the rate of action of any named enzyme?",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Why are enzymes frequently referred to as ‘biological catalysts'?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Enzymes are frequently referred to as biological catalysts because they speed up the rate of chemical reactions in living organisms",
                     "points":4
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"What would be the effect of changing: i) pH? ii) Temperature upon the rate of action of any named enzyme?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "subQuestions":[
                        {
                           "label":"i)",
                           "text":"pH",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Changing the pH of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum pH at which they work. When the pH is changed, the enzymes change shape so that their substrate cannot fit in their active sites. This is called enzyme denaturation",
                           "points":1
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"ii)",
                           "text":"Temperature",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Changing the temperature of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum temperature at which they work. When the temperature is changed, the enzymes will be denatured",
                           "points":1
                        }
                     ],
                     "correctAnswer":"i) Changing the pH of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum pH at which they work. When the pH is changed, the enzymes change shape so that their substrate cannot fit in their active sites. This is called enzyme denaturation. ii) Changing the temperature of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum temperature at which they work. When the temperature is changed, the enzymes will be denatured",
                     "points":2
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) Enzymes are frequently referred to as biological catalysts because they speed up the rate of chemical reactions in living organisms. b) i) Changing the pH of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum pH at which they work. When the pH is changed, the enzymes change shape so that their substrate cannot fit in their active sites. This is called enzyme denaturation. ii) Changing the temperature of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum temperature at which they work. When the temperature is changed, the enzymes will be denatured",
               "points":6
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":9,
               "text":"The diagram below shows blood supply through the liver.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Label the parts indicated A, B, C, D, E.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"A: vena cava; B: aorta; C: hepatic vein; D: hepatic artery; E: hepatic portal vein",
                     "points":5
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"Which two substances would be present in greater concentrations in vessel E after a meal?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Glucose, amino acids",
                     "points":2
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) A: vena cava; B: aorta; C: hepatic vein; D: hepatic artery; E: hepatic portal vein. b) Glucose, amino acids",
               "points":7
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":10,
               "text":"Give at least four functions of a human brain.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "correctAnswer":"Thinking, reasoning, storage of information, memory, love, imagination, vision",
               "points":4
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":11,
               "text":"When a person's hand accidentally touches a hot object it is quickly withdrawn. Explain what causes this response.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "correctAnswer":"It is a reflex action. A reflex action is an automatic response to a stimulus which is not under the control of the brain. Reflexes are controlled by the spinal cord. The command from the spinal cord makes the muscles of the hand to contract, removing the hand from the hot object (stimulus)",
               "points":3
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":12,
               "text":"a) Define a gamete. b) What are the male and female gametes in an animal (mammal)?",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Define a gamete.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"A gamete is a cell involved in sexual reproduction",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"What are the male and female gametes in an animal (mammal)?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"The male gamete is the sperm. The female gamete is the egg",
                     "points":2
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) A gamete is a cell involved in sexual reproduction. b) The male gamete is the sperm. The female gamete is the egg",
               "points":3
            }
         ]
      },
      {
         "name":"B",
         "description":"Answer any THREE questions from this section. (30 marks)",
         "questions":[
            {
               "questionNumber":13,
               "text":"a) Explain the following terms that are used in genetics: i) Allele ii) Heterozygous iii) Phenotype b) In humans, brown eye (B) is dominant to blue eye (b). Two parents, one heterozygous for eye colour and the other blue eyes, start a family.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Explain the following terms that are used in genetics:",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "subQuestions":[
                        {
                           "label":"i)",
                           "text":"Allele",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Alleles are different forms of the same gene",
                           "points":1
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"ii)",
                           "text":"Heterozygous",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Heterozygous: having different alleles for a given character",
                           "points":1
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"iii)",
                           "text":"Phenotype",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Phenotype: the physical appearance of an organism",
                           "points":1
                        }
                     ],
                     "correctAnswer":"i) Alleles are different forms of the same gene. ii) Heterozygous: having different alleles for a given character. iii) Phenotype: the physical appearance of an organism",
                     "points":3
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"In humans, brown eye (B) is dominant to blue eye (b). Two parents, one heterozygous for eye colour and the other blue eyes, start a family.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "subQuestions":[
                        {
                           "label":"i)",
                           "text":"What is the genotype of the brown-eyed parent?",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Bb",
                           "points":1
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"ii)",
                           "text":"What are the possible gametes that each parent can produce?",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"B and b from the parent with genotype Bb and b from the parent with genotype bb",
                           "points":2
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"iii)",
                           "text":"Show the possible genotypes of their children.",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"50% Bb; 50% bb",
                           "points":4
                        }
                     ],
                     "correctAnswer":"i) Bb. ii) B and b from the parent with genotype Bb and b from the parent with genotype bb. iii) 50% Bb; 50% bb",
                     "points":7
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) i) Alleles are different forms of the same gene. ii) Heterozygous: having different alleles for a given character. iii) Phenotype: the physical appearance of an organism. b) i) Bb. ii) B and b from the parent with genotype Bb and b from the parent with genotype bb. iii) 50% Bb; 50% bb",
               "points":10
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":14,
               "text":"a) Explain the following terms that are used in ecology: i) Biosphere ii) Habitat iii) Niche iv) Producer b) Answer the following questions in relation to the food web shown below.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Explain the following terms that are used in ecology:",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "subQuestions":[
                        {
                           "label":"i)",
                           "text":"Biosphere",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Biosphere is the place on Earth where life is possible",
                           "points":1
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"ii)",
                           "text":"Habitat",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Habitat is the place where an organism lives",
                           "points":1
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"iii)",
                           "text":"Niche",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Niche is the role of the species in the ecosystem",
                           "points":1
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"iv)",
                           "text":"Producer",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Producer: is an organism that can make its own food",
                           "points":1
                        }
                     ],
                     "correctAnswer":"i) Biosphere is the place on Earth where life is possible. ii) Habitat is the place where an organism lives. iii) Niche is the role of the species in the ecosystem. iv) Producer: is an organism that can make its own food",
                     "points":4
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"Answer the following questions in relation to the food web shown below.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "subQuestions":[
                        {
                           "label":"i)",
                           "text":"Write two food chains with four organisms in it.",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Ash tree — Caterpillars — Robins — Owls; Ash tree — Earthworms — Robins — Owls",
                           "points":2
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"ii)",
                           "text":"Copy and complete the table below.",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Autotrophic organism: Ash tree. Secondary consumer: Robins, ladybirds. Omnivore: Robins. Carnivore: Owls",
                           "points":4
                        }
                     ],
                     "correctAnswer":"i) Ash tree — Caterpillars — Robins — Owls; Ash tree — Earthworms — Robins — Owls. ii) Autotrophic organism: Ash tree. Secondary consumer: Robins, ladybirds. Omnivore: Robins. Carnivore: Owls",
                     "points":6
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) i) Biosphere is the place on Earth where life is possible. ii) Habitat is the place where an organism lives. iii) Niche is the role of the species in the ecosystem. iv) Producer: is an organism that can make its own food. b) i) Ash tree — Caterpillars — Robins — Owls; Ash tree — Earthworms — Robins — Owls. ii) Autotrophic organism: Ash tree. Secondary consumer: Robins, ladybirds. Omnivore: Robins. Carnivore: Owls",
               "points":10
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":15,
               "text":"a) Describe the effects of the failure of the pancreas to produce sufficient insulin. b) The pituitary gland produces several hormones.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Describe the effects of the failure of the pancreas to produce sufficient insulin.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"The failure of the pancreas to produce sufficient insulin will lead to a disorder called diabetes mellitus",
                     "points":5
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"The pituitary gland produces several hormones.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "subQuestions":[
                        {
                           "label":"i)",
                           "text":"Give any four (4) hormones it produces.",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Growth hormone (GH), TSH, ACTH, prolactin, FSH, LH",
                           "points":4
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"ii)",
                           "text":"Give the functions of each hormone named in (b) (i) above.",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Growth hormone (GH): Stimulates growth especially of bones and limbs and metabolic functions. TSH: Synthesis and secretion of thyroid hormones, growth of thyroid glands. ACTH: Stimulates the adrenal cortex to secrete its hormones. Prolactin: stimulates milk production and secretion. FSH: In male, stimulates spermatogenesis. In female, growth of ovarian follicles. LH: In male, testosterone production. In female, secretion of oestrogens and progesterone, ovulation and maintenance of corpus luteum",
                           "points":4
                        }
                     ],
                     "correctAnswer":"i) Growth hormone (GH), TSH, ACTH, prolactin, FSH, LH. ii) Growth hormone (GH): Stimulates growth especially of bones and limbs and metabolic functions. TSH: Synthesis and secretion of thyroid hormones, growth of thyroid glands. ACTH: Stimulates the adrenal cortex to secrete its hormones. Prolactin: stimulates milk production and secretion. FSH: In male, stimulates spermatogenesis. In female, growth of ovarian follicles. LH: In male, testosterone production. In female, secretion of oestrogens and progesterone, ovulation and maintenance of corpus luteum",
                     "points":8
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) The failure of the pancreas to produce sufficient insulin will lead to a disorder called diabetes mellitus. b) i) Growth hormone (GH), TSH, ACTH, prolactin, FSH, LH. ii) Growth hormone (GH): Stimulates growth especially of bones and limbs and metabolic functions. TSH: Synthesis and secretion of thyroid hormones, growth of thyroid glands. ACTH: Stimulates the adrenal cortex to secrete its hormones. Prolactin: stimulates milk production and secretion. FSH: In male, stimulates spermatogenesis. In female, growth of ovarian follicles. LH: In male, testosterone production. In female, secretion of oestrogens and progesterone, ovulation and maintenance of corpus luteum",
               "points":13
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":16,
               "text":"The diagram below represents the events leading up to fertilization, implantation and development in human beings.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Name the structures labelled A, B, C, D, E, F and G.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"A: vagina; B: cervix; C: amniotic sac; D: uterus; E: placenta; F: oviduct; G: foetus",
                     "points":4
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"Briefly describe the process of fertilization in human beings, from (i) to (vi).",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"(i): Growth of an ovarian follicle. (ii): The ovary ruptures to release an egg (ovulation). (iii): Sperm are introduced in the vagina during sexual intercourse. (iv): The egg moves toward the oviduct. (v): The sperm meets the egg and one of the sperm fertilizes the egg forming a diploid zygote. The zygote spends the next few days traveling down the Fallopian tube. As it travels, it divides by mitosis several times to form a ball of cells called a morula. The cell divisions, which are called cleavage, increase the number of cells but not their overall size. More cell divisions occur, and soon a fluid-filled cavity forms inside the ball of cells. At this stage, the ball of cells is called a blastocyst. (vi): The blastocyst embeds in the wall of the uterus. This is called implantation",
                     "points":6
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) A: vagina; B: cervix; C: amniotic sac; D: uterus; E: placenta; F: oviduct; G: foetus. b) (i): Growth of an ovarian follicle. (ii): The ovary ruptures to release an egg (ovulation). (iii): Sperm are introduced in the vagina during sexual intercourse. (iv): The egg moves toward the oviduct. (v): The sperm meets the egg and one of the sperm fertilizes the egg forming a diploid zygote. The zygote spends the next few days traveling down the Fallopian tube. As it travels, it divides by mitosis several times to form a ball of cells called a morula. The cell divisions, which are called cleavage, increase the number of cells but not their overall size. More cell divisions occur, and soon a fluid-filled cavity forms inside the ball of cells. At this stage, the ball of cells is called a blastocyst. (vi): The blastocyst embeds in the wall of the uterus. This is called implantation",
               "points":10
            }
         ]
      },
      {
         "name":"C",
         "description":"This question is compulsory. (15 marks)",
         "questions":[
            {
               "questionNumber":17,
               "text":"a) State one function of each of the following: i) Petal ii) Sepal b) What is the difference between self-pollination and cross-pollination? c) Some species of plants are strongly adapted to pollination by certain insects. State four characteristics which are regarded as adaptations to pollination by bees. d) In dicotyledonous plants, the early stages of germination take place in the soil where there is little or no light for photosynthesis. How does the seedling obtain materials for its growth and energy during this time?",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"State one function of each of the following:",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "subQuestions":[
                        {
                           "label":"i)",
                           "text":"Petal",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Attract pollinators such as bees and insects",
                           "points":1
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"ii)",
                           "text":"Sepal",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Protection of inner parts of the flower (reproductive organs)",
                           "points":1
                        }
                     ],
                     "correctAnswer":"i) Attract pollinators such as bees and insects. ii) Protection of inner parts of the flower (reproductive organs)",
                     "points":2
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"What is the difference between self-pollination and cross-pollination?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Self-pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anthers to the stigma of the same flower. Cross-pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anthers of one flower to the stigma of another flower of another plant of the same species",
                     "points":2
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"c)",
                     "text":"Some species of plants are strongly adapted to pollination by certain insects. State four characteristics which are regarded as adaptations to pollination by bees.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Are scented (have a pleasant strong smell). Are large and conspicuous (very noticeable or tending to attract attention). Pollen grains are rough, sticky on insect’s body. Are brightly coloured. Produce nectar",
                     "points":4
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"d)",
                     "text":"In dicotyledonous plants, the early stages of germination take place in the soil where there is little or no light for photosynthesis. How does the seedling obtain materials for its growth and energy during this time?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"The seedling obtains materials and energy from its growth from the endosperm. The endosperm contains storage molecules which are hydrolysed into simple monomers to be used by the seedling before it starts producing food by its own",
                     "points":2
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) i) Attract pollinators such as bees and insects. ii) Protection of inner parts of the flower (reproductive organs). b) Self-pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anthers to the stigma of the same flower. Cross-pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anthers of one flower to the stigma of another flower of another plant of the same species. c) Are scented (have a pleasant strong smell). Are large and conspicuous (very noticeable or tending to attract attention). Pollen grains are rough, sticky on insect’s body. Are brightly coloured. Produce nectar. d) The seedling obtains materials and energy from its growth from the endosperm. The endosperm contains storage molecules which are hydrolysed into simple monomers to be used by the seedling before it starts producing food by its own",
               "points":9
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":18,
               "text":"The diagram below represents molecules of a salt dissolved in the bottom layer of water in a beaker.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"After a few minutes.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"A diagram showing salt molecules that are moving from the bottom upwards (salt molecules are moving from their highest concentration to their lowest concentration)",
                     "points":2
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"The diagram below shows a vessel which contains a concentrated (right) and a dilute (left) solution separated by a partially permeable membrane.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "subQuestions":[
                        {
                           "label":"i)",
                           "text":"Draw a similar diagram to show the liquid levels after an hour or two hours.",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Water molecules have moved from hypotonic solution to hypertonic solution through a selectively permeable membrane",
                           "points":2
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"ii)",
                           "text":"Explain what has happened.",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Water always moves from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane",
                           "points":4
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"iii)",
                           "text":"Name the process which is being investigated.",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Osmosis",
                           "points":1
                        },
                        {
                           "label":"iv)",
                           "text":"Define the process named in (iii) above.",
                           "type":"open-ended",
                           "correctAnswer":"Osmosis is a movement of water molecules from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane",
                           "points":3
                        }
                     ],
                     "correctAnswer":"i) Water molecules have moved from hypotonic solution to hypertonic solution through a selectively permeable membrane. ii) Water always moves from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane. iii) Osmosis. iv) Osmosis is a movement of water molecules from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane",
                     "points":10
                  }
               ],
               "correctAnswer":"a) A diagram showing salt molecules that are moving from the bottom upwards (salt molecules are moving from their highest concentration to their lowest concentration). b) i) Water molecules have moved from hypotonic solution to hypertonic solution through a selectively permeable membrane. ii) Water always moves from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane. iii) Osmosis. iv) Osmosis is a movement of water molecules from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane",
               "points":12
            }
         ]
      }
   ]
}

parsedContent.instructions: Write your names and index number on the answer booklet as they appear on your registration form, and DO NOT write your names and index number on additional answer sheets of paper if provided. Do not open this paper until you are told to do so. This paper consists of three sections A, B and C. Answer ALL questions in section A. Answer THREE questions in section B. Answer only one question in section C. Use only a blue or black pen.

answerKey.1: a) Organelle, cell, tissue, organ, organ system; b) i. Organism; ii. Organism (or cell); iii. Organ; iv. Organelle; v. Organ system
answerKey.2: a) Receptor cells; b) Sensory neurons; c) Motor neurons
answerKey.3: a) Larva stage and adult stage; b) Larva stage; c) Pupa stage; d) Pupa stage
answerKey.4: Drinking bottled water, proper sanitation, proper disposal of wastes, covering food, washing hands before eating
answerKey.5: a) Photosynthesis is a metabolic process by which plants and other autotrophic organisms make their own food using water and carbon dioxide in the presence of sunlight and chlorophyll. b) Conditions necessary for photosynthesis: presence of water, carbon dioxide, suitable temperature, presence of chlorophyll or chloroplasts
answerKey.6: a) Magnesium, iron; b) i. In green plants: magnesium is required in the synthesis of chlorophyll; ii. In mammals: iron is used in the synthesis of haemoglobin
answerKey.7: a) Community: A number of species interacting in a locality; b) Ecosystem: Interaction of organisms with each other and with their abiotic environment; c) Food web: A nutritional interrelationship of organisms; d) Habitat: A place where an organism lives
answerKey.8: a) Enzymes are frequently referred to as biological catalysts because they speed up the rate of chemical reactions in living organisms. b) Changing the pH of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum pH at which they work. When the pH is changed, the enzymes change shape so that their substrate cannot fit in their active sites. This is called enzyme denaturation. c) Changing the temperature of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum temperature at which they work. When the temperature is changed, the enzymes will be denatured
answerKey.9: a) A: vena cava; B: aorta; C: hepatic vein; D: hepatic artery; E: hepatic portal vein; b) Glucose, amino acids
answerKey.10: Thinking, reasoning, storage of information, memory, love, imagination, vision
answerKey.11: It is a reflex action. A reflex action is an automatic response to a stimulus which is not under the control of the brain. Reflexes are controlled by the spinal cord. The command from the spinal cord makes the muscles of the hand to contract, removing the hand from the hot object (stimulus)
answerKey.12: a) A gamete is a cell involved in sexual reproduction. b) The male gamete is the sperm. The female gamete is the egg
answerKey.13: a) i. Alleles are different forms of the same gene. ii. Heterozygous: having different alleles for a given character. iii. Phenotype: the physical appearance of an organism. b) Brown eye is dominant: B; Blue eyes is recessive: b. Cross between two parents: one heterozygous (Bb) and the other with blue eyes (bb). i. Genotype of the brown-eyed parent: Bb. ii. The possible gametes that each parent can produce: B and b from the parent with genotype Bb and b from the parent with genotype bb. iii. The possible genotypes of their children: 50% Bb; 50% bb
answerKey.14: a) i. Biosphere is the place on Earth where life is possible. ii. Habitat is the place where an organism lives. iii. Niche is the role of the species in the ecosystem. iv. Producer: is an organism that can make its own food. b) i. Ash tree — Caterpillars — Robins — Owls; ii. Ash tree — Earthworms — Robins — Owls. Autotrophic organism: Ash tree. Secondary consumer: Robins, ladybirds. Omnivore: Robins. Carnivore: Owls
answerKey.15: a) The failure of the pancreas to produce sufficient insulin will lead to a disorder called diabetes mellitus. b) i. Growth hormone (GH), TSH, ACTH, prolactin, FSH, LH. Growth hormone (GH): Stimulates growth especially of bones and limbs and metabolic functions. TSH: Synthesis and secretion of thyroid hormones, growth of thyroid glands. ACTH: Stimulates the adrenal cortex to secrete its hormones. Prolactin: stimulates milk production and secretion. FSH: In male, stimulates spermatogenesis. In female, growth of ovarian follicles. LH: In male, testosterone production. In female, secretion of oestrogens and progesterone, ovulation and maintenance of corpus luteum
answerKey.16: a) A: vagina; B: cervix; C: amniotic sac; D: uterus; E: placenta; F: oviduct; G: foetus. b) (i): Growth of an ovarian follicle. (ii): The ovary ruptures to release an egg (ovulation). (iii): Sperm are introduced in the vagina during sexual intercourse. (iv): The egg moves toward the oviduct. (v): The sperm meets the egg and one of the sperm fertilizes the egg forming a diploid zygote. The zygote spends the next few days traveling down the Fallopian tube. As it travels, it divides by mitosis several times to form a ball of cells called a morula. The cell divisions, which are called cleavage, increase the number of cells but not their overall size. More cell divisions occur, and soon a fluid-filled cavity forms inside the ball of cells. At this stage, the ball of cells is called a blastocyst. (vi): The blastocyst embeds in the wall of the uterus. This is called implantation
answerKey.17: a) i. Sepals: protection of inner parts of the flower (reproductive organs). ii. Petals: attract pollinators such as bees and insects. b) Self-pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anthers to the stigma of the same flower. Cross-pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anthers of one flower to the stigma of another flower of another plant of the same species. c) Are scented (have a pleasant strong smell). Are large and conspicuous (very noticeable or tending to attract attention). Pollen grains are rough, sticky on insect’s body. Are brightly coloured. Produce nectar. d) The seedling obtains materials and energy from its growth from the endosperm. The endosperm contains storage molecules which are hydrolysed into simple monomers to be used by the seedling before it starts producing food by its own
answerKey.18: a) A diagram showing salt molecules that are moving from the bottom upwards (salt molecules are moving from their highest concentration to their lowest concentration). A diagram showing salt molecules with a uniform distribution in the water. iii. Diffusion. b) Membrane. Water molecules have moved from hypotonic solution to hypertonic solution through a selectively permeable membrane. This is because water always moves from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane. iii. Osmosis. iv. Osmosis is a movement of water molecules from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane

sections.name: A
sections.description: Attempt all questions from this section. (55 marks)

questions.text: The following are various sub-units into which species of plants or animals can be divided: cell, organ, organelle, organism, tissue, organ system.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Arrange them in order starting with the simplest and ending with the most complex.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Organelle, cell, tissue, organ, organ system

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Which of them can be applied to the following:

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: A cat?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Organism

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Amoeba?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Organism (or cell)

subQuestions.label: iii)
subQuestions.text: Leaf?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Organ

subQuestions.label: iv)
subQuestions.text: Chloroplast?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Organelle

subQuestions.label: v)
subQuestions.text: Alimentary canal?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Organ system
i) Organism; ii) Organism (or cell); iii) Organ; iv) Organelle; v) Organ system

questions.correctAnswer: a) Organelle, cell, tissue, organ, organ system; b) i. Organism; ii. Organism (or cell); iii. Organ; iv. Organelle; v. Organ system

questions.text: Name the cells in a mammal that:

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Are sensitive to their environment.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Receptor cells

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Receive messages from sensory cells,
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Sensory neurons

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Transmit messages to effectors.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Motor neurons

questions.correctAnswer: a) Receptor cells; b) Sensory neurons; c) Motor neurons

questions.text: Name the stage in the life cycle of flies and butterflies at which:

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Ecdysis occurs.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Larva stage and adult stage

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Feeding for growth occurs.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Larva stage

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Wings appear,
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Pupa stage

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: A period of immobility occurs.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Pupa stage

questions.correctAnswer: a) Larva stage and adult stage; b) Larva stage; c) Pupa stage; d) Pupa stage

questions.text: Cholera is transmitted by food and water that is contaminated with faecal matter. Suggest three measures that might be used to limit the spread of this disease.
questions.correctAnswer: Drinking bottled water, proper sanitation, proper disposal of wastes, covering food, washing hands before eating

questions.text: a) Define photosynthesis. b) What conditions are necessary for photosynthesis to occur in a green plant?

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Define photosynthesis.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Photosynthesis is a metabolic process by which plants and other autotrophic organisms make their own food using water and carbon dioxide in the presence of sunlight and chlorophyll

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: What conditions are necessary for photosynthesis to occur in a green plant?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Presence of water, carbon dioxide, suitable temperature, presence of chlorophyll or chloroplasts

questions.correctAnswer: a) Photosynthesis is a metabolic process by which plants and other autotrophic organisms make their own food using water and carbon dioxide in the presence of sunlight and chlorophyll. b) Presence of water, carbon dioxide, suitable temperature, presence of chlorophyll or chloroplasts

questions.text: a) List two chemical elements other than carbon, hydrogen, oxygen and nitrogen which are required by both plants and animals. b) For each of the elements you have listed, give one reason why it is required:

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: List two chemical elements other than carbon, hydrogen, oxygen and nitrogen which are required by both plants and animals.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Magnesium, iron

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: For each of the elements you have listed, give one reason why it is required:

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: In green plants.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Magnesium is required in the synthesis of chlorophyll

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: In mammals.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Iron is used in the synthesis of haemoglobin
i) Magnesium is required in the synthesis of chlorophyll; ii) Iron is used in the synthesis of haemoglobin

questions.correctAnswer: a) Magnesium, iron. b) i) Magnesium is required in the synthesis of chlorophyll; ii) Iron is used in the synthesis of haemoglobin

questions.text: Match the following ecological terms with their definitions.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Community
subQuestions.correctAnswer: A number of species interacting in a locality

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Ecosystem
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Interaction of organisms with each other and with their abiotic environment

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Food web
subQuestions.correctAnswer: A nutritional interrelationship of organisms

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: Habitat
subQuestions.correctAnswer: A place where an organism lives

questions.correctAnswer: a) Community: A number of species interacting in a locality; b) Ecosystem: Interaction of organisms with each other and with their abiotic environment; c) Food web: A nutritional interrelationship of organisms; d) Habitat: A place where an organism lives

questions.text: a) Why are enzymes frequently referred to as ‘biological catalysts'? b) What would be the effect of changing: i) pH? ii) Temperature upon the rate of action of any named enzyme?

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Why are enzymes frequently referred to as ‘biological catalysts'?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Enzymes are frequently referred to as biological catalysts because they speed up the rate of chemical reactions in living organisms

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: What would be the effect of changing: i) pH? ii) Temperature upon the rate of action of any named enzyme?

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: pH
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Changing the pH of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum pH at which they work. When the pH is changed, the enzymes change shape so that their substrate cannot fit in their active sites. This is called enzyme denaturation

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Temperature
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Changing the temperature of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum temperature at which they work. When the temperature is changed, the enzymes will be denatured

i) Changing the pH of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum pH at which they work. When the pH is changed, the enzymes change shape so that their substrate cannot fit in their active sites. This is called enzyme denaturation. ii) Changing the temperature of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum temperature at which they work. When the temperature is changed, the enzymes will be denatured

questions.correctAnswer: a) Enzymes are frequently referred to as biological catalysts because they speed up the rate of chemical reactions in living organisms. b) i) Changing the pH of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum pH at which they work. When the pH is changed, the enzymes change shape so that their substrate cannot fit in their active sites. This is called enzyme denaturation. ii) Changing the temperature of an enzyme-controlled reaction will affect the enzyme. Enzymes have an optimum temperature at which they work. When the temperature is changed, the enzymes will be denatured

questions.text: The diagram below shows blood supply through the liver.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Label the parts indicated A, B, C, D, E.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: A: vena cava; B: aorta; C: hepatic vein; D: hepatic artery; E: hepatic portal vein

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Which two substances would be present in greater concentrations in vessel E after a meal?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Glucose, amino acids

questions.correctAnswer: a) A: vena cava; B: aorta; C: hepatic vein; D: hepatic artery; E: hepatic portal vein. b) Glucose, amino acids

questions.text: Give at least four functions of a human brain.
questions.correctAnswer: Thinking, reasoning, storage of information, memory, love, imagination, vision

questions.text: When a person's hand accidentally touches a hot object it is quickly withdrawn. Explain what causes this response.
questions.correctAnswer: It is a reflex action. A reflex action is an automatic response to a stimulus which is not under the control of the brain. Reflexes are controlled by the spinal cord. The command from the spinal cord makes the muscles of the hand to contract, removing the hand from the hot object (stimulus)

questions.text: a) Define a gamete. b) What are the male and female gametes in an animal (mammal)?

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Define a gamete.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: A gamete is a cell involved in sexual reproduction

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: What are the male and female gametes in an animal (mammal)?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: The male gamete is the sperm. The female gamete is the egg

questions.correctAnswer: a) A gamete is a cell involved in sexual reproduction. b) The male gamete is the sperm. The female gamete is the egg

sections.name: B
sections.description: Answer any THREE questions from this section. (30 marks)

questions.text: a) Explain the following terms that are used in genetics: i) Allele ii) Heterozygous iii) Phenotype b) In humans, brown eye (B) is dominant to blue eye (b). Two parents, one heterozygous for eye colour and the other blue eyes, start a family.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Explain the following terms that are used in genetics:

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Allele
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Alleles are different forms of the same gene

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Heterozygous
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Heterozygous: having different alleles for a given character

subQuestions.label: iii)
subQuestions.text: Phenotype
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Phenotype: the physical appearance of an organism
i) Alleles are different forms of the same gene. ii) Heterozygous: having different alleles for a given character. iii) Phenotype: the physical appearance of an organism

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: In humans, brown eye (B) is dominant to blue eye (b). Two parents, one heterozygous for eye colour and the other blue eyes, start a family.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: What is the genotype of the brown-eyed parent?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Bb

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: What are the possible gametes that each parent can produce?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: B and b from the parent with genotype Bb and b from the parent with genotype bb

subQuestions.label: iii)
subQuestions.text: Show the possible genotypes of their children.

subQuestions.correctAnswer: i) Bb. ii) B and b from the parent with genotype Bb and b from the parent with genotype bb. iii) 50% Bb; 50% bb

questions.correctAnswer: a) i) Alleles are different forms of the same gene. ii) Heterozygous: having different alleles for a given character. iii) Phenotype: the physical appearance of an organism. b) i) Bb. ii) B and b from the parent with genotype Bb and b from the parent with genotype bb. iii) 50% Bb; 50% bb

questions.text: a) Explain the following terms that are used in ecology: i) Biosphere ii) Habitat iii) Niche iv) Producer b) Answer the following questions in relation to the food web shown below.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Explain the following terms that are used in ecology:

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Biosphere
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Biosphere is the place on Earth where life is possible

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Habitat
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Habitat is the place where an organism lives

subQuestions.label: iii)
subQuestions.text: Niche
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Niche is the role of the species in the ecosystem

subQuestions.label: iv)
subQuestions.text: Producer
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Producer: is an organism that can make its own food

i) Biosphere is the place on Earth where life is possible. ii) Habitat is the place where an organism lives. iii) Niche is the role of the species in the ecosystem. iv) Producer: is an organism that can make its own food

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Answer the following questions in relation to the food web shown below.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Write two food chains with four organisms in it.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Ash tree — Caterpillars — Robins — Owls; Ash tree — Earthworms — Robins — Owls

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Copy and complete the table below.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Autotrophic organism: Ash tree. Secondary consumer: Robins, ladybirds. Omnivore: Robins. Carnivore: Owls
i) Ash tree — Caterpillars — Robins — Owls; Ash tree — Earthworms — Robins — Owls. ii) Autotrophic organism: Ash tree. Secondary consumer: Robins, ladybirds. Omnivore: Robins. Carnivore: Owls

questions.correctAnswer: a) i) Biosphere is the place on Earth where life is possible. ii) Habitat is the place where an organism lives. iii) Niche is the role of the species in the ecosystem. iv) Producer: is an organism that can make its own food. b) i) Ash tree — Caterpillars — Robins — Owls; Ash tree — Earthworms — Robins — Owls. ii) Autotrophic organism: Ash tree. Secondary consumer: Robins, ladybirds. Omnivore: Robins. Carnivore: Owls

questions.text: a) Describe the effects of the failure of the pancreas to produce sufficient insulin. b) The pituitary gland produces several hormones.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Describe the effects of the failure of the pancreas to produce sufficient insulin.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: The failure of the pancreas to produce sufficient insulin will lead to a disorder called diabetes mellitus

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: The pituitary gland produces several hormones.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Give any four (4) hormones it produces.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Growth hormone (GH), TSH, ACTH, prolactin, FSH, LH

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Give the functions of each hormone named in (b) (i) above.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Growth hormone (GH): Stimulates growth especially of bones and limbs and metabolic functions. TSH: Synthesis and secretion of thyroid hormones, growth of thyroid glands. ACTH: Stimulates the adrenal cortex to secrete its hormones. Prolactin: stimulates milk production and secretion. FSH: In male, stimulates spermatogenesis. In female, growth of ovarian follicles. LH: In male, testosterone production. In female, secretion of oestrogens and progesterone, ovulation and maintenance of corpus luteum

i) Growth hormone (GH), TSH, ACTH, prolactin, FSH, LH. ii) Growth hormone (GH): Stimulates growth especially of bones and limbs and metabolic functions. TSH: Synthesis and secretion of thyroid hormones, growth of thyroid glands. ACTH: Stimulates the adrenal cortex to secrete its hormones. Prolactin: stimulates milk production and secretion. FSH: In male, stimulates spermatogenesis. In female, growth of ovarian follicles. LH: In male, testosterone production. In female, secretion of oestrogens and progesterone, ovulation and maintenance of corpus luteum

TSH: Synthesis and secretion of thyroid hormones, growth of thyroid glands. ACTH: Stimulates the adrenal cortex to secrete its hormones. Prolactin: stimulates milk production and secretion. FSH: In male, stimulates spermatogenesis. In female, growth of ovarian follicles. LH: In male, testosterone production. In female, secretion of oestrogens and progesterone, ovulation and maintenance of corpus luteum

questions.text: The diagram below represents the events leading up to fertilization, implantation and development in human beings.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Name the structures labelled A, B, C, D, E, F and G.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: A: vagina; B: cervix; C: amniotic sac; D: uterus; E: placenta; F: oviduct; G: foetus

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Briefly describe the process of fertilization in human beings, from (i) to (vi).
subQuestions.correctAnswer: (i): Growth of an ovarian follicle. (ii): The ovary ruptures to release an egg (ovulation). (iii): Sperm are introduced in the vagina during sexual intercourse. (iv): The egg moves toward the oviduct. (v): The sperm meets the egg and one of the sperm fertilizes the egg forming a diploid zygote. The zygote spends the next few days traveling down the Fallopian tube. As it travels, it divides by mitosis several times to form a ball of cells called a morula. The cell divisions, which are called cleavage, increase the number of cells but not their overall size. More cell divisions occur, and soon a fluid-filled cavity forms inside the ball of cells. At this stage, the ball of cells is called a blastocyst. (vi): The blastocyst embeds in the wall of the uterus. This is called implantation

questions.correctAnswer: a) A: vagina; B: cervix; C: amniotic sac; D: uterus; E: placenta; F: oviduct; G: foetus. b) (i): Growth of an ovarian follicle. (ii): The ovary ruptures to release an egg (ovulation). (iii): Sperm are introduced in the vagina during sexual intercourse. (iv): The egg moves toward the oviduct. (v): The sperm meets the egg and one of the sperm fertilizes the egg forming a diploid zygote. The zygote spends the next few days traveling down the Fallopian tube. As it travels, it divides by mitosis several times to form a ball of cells called a morula. The cell divisions, which are called cleavage, increase the number of cells but not their overall size. More cell divisions occur, and soon a fluid-filled cavity forms inside the ball of cells. At this stage, the ball of cells is called a blastocyst. (vi): The blastocyst embeds in the wall of the uterus. This is called implantation

questions.text: a) State one function of each of the following: i) Petal ii) Sepal b) What is the difference between self-pollination and cross-pollination? c) Some species of plants are strongly adapted to pollination by certain insects. State four characteristics which are regarded as adaptations to pollination by bees. d) In dicotyledonous plants, the early stages of germination take place in the soil where there is little or no light for photosynthesis. How does the seedling obtain materials for its growth and energy during this time?

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: State one function of each of the following:

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Petal
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Attract pollinators such as bees and insects

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Sepal
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Protection of inner parts of the flower (reproductive organs)
i) Attract pollinators such as bees and insects. ii) Protection of inner parts of the flower (reproductive organs)

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: What is the difference between self-pollination and cross-pollination?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Self-pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anthers to the stigma of the same flower. Cross-pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anthers of one flower to the stigma of another flower of another plant of the same species

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Some species of plants are strongly adapted to pollination by certain insects. State four characteristics which are regarded as adaptations to pollination by bees.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Are scented (have a pleasant strong smell). Are large and conspicuous (very noticeable or tending to attract attention). Pollen grains are rough, sticky on insect’s body. Are brightly coloured. Produce nectar

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: In dicotyledonous plants, the early stages of germination take place in the soil where there is little or no light for photosynthesis. How does the seedling obtain materials for its growth and energy during this time?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: The seedling obtains materials and energy from its growth from the endosperm. The endosperm contains storage molecules which are hydrolysed into simple monomers to be used by the seedling before it starts producing food by its own

questions.correctAnswer: a) i) Attract pollinators such as bees and insects. ii) Protection of inner parts of the flower (reproductive organs). b) Self-pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anthers to the stigma of the same flower. Cross-pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anthers of one flower to the stigma of another flower of another plant of the same species. c) Are scented (have a pleasant strong smell). Are large and conspicuous (very noticeable or tending to attract attention). Pollen grains are rough, sticky on insect’s body. Are brightly coloured. Produce nectar. d) The seedling obtains materials and energy from its growth from the endosperm. The endosperm contains storage molecules which are hydrolysed into simple monomers to be used by the seedling before it starts producing food by its own

questions.text: The diagram below represents molecules of a salt dissolved in the bottom layer of water in a beaker.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: After a few minutes.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: A diagram showing salt molecules that are moving from the bottom upwards (salt molecules are moving from their highest concentration to their lowest concentration)

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: The diagram below shows a vessel which contains a concentrated (right) and a dilute (left) solution separated by a partially permeable membrane.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Draw a similar diagram to show the liquid levels after an hour or two hours.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Water molecules have moved from hypotonic solution to hypertonic solution through a selectively permeable membrane

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Explain what has happened.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Water always moves from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane

subQuestions.label: iii)
subQuestions.text: Name the process which is being investigated.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Osmosis

subQuestions.label: iv)
subQuestions.text: Define the process named in (iii) above.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Osmosis is a movement of water molecules from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane

i) Water molecules have moved from hypotonic solution to hypertonic solution through a selectively permeable membrane. ii) Water always moves from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane. iii) Osmosis. iv) Osmosis is a movement of water molecules from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane

questions.correctAnswer: a) A diagram showing salt molecules that are moving from the bottom upwards (salt molecules are moving from their highest concentration to their lowest concentration). b) i) Water molecules have moved from hypotonic solution to hypertonic solution through a selectively permeable membrane. ii) Water always moves from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane. iii) Osmosis. iv) Osmosis is a movement of water molecules from a medium with high water potential (hypotonic solution) to a medium with a low water potential (hypertonic solution) through a selectively permeable membrane
